heading: ## Limitations
body:
- Single-centre design limits generalisability to other healthcare settings and populations.
- Short follow-up (6 months) may not capture long-term effects on patient activation or clinical outcomes.
- Self-reported PAM-13 scores are subject to recall bias and social desirability bias.
- Digital literacy requirements may exclude patients with limited technology experience, introducing selection bias.
- Hawthorne effect from monitoring may inflate observed improvements in both arms.
- Placebo effect from interaction with an AI system cannot be fully controlled.
- Assumed standard deviation (σ = 10) may not hold in the target population; sensitivity analysis partially addresses this.
- Reference integrity issues (see audit) may affect credibility of background justification.

heading: ## Risk matrix
body:
| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-------------|--------|------------|
| Recruitment failure | Medium | High | Multi-site recruitment, extended timeline |
| LLM hallucination | Medium | High | Human-in-the-loop review, automated fact-checking |
| Data privacy breach | Low | Critical | Encryption, access controls, GDPR compliance |
| PAM-13 license denial | Low | High | Alternative activation measure (e.g., Patient Activation Measure short form) |
| Reference integrity errors | High | Medium | Systematic reference audit, DOI verification |
| Dropout >20% | Medium | Medium | Over-recruitment (N=132), retention incentives |
| σ estimate inaccurate | Medium | Medium | Sensitivity analysis, adaptive sample size re-estimation |

replace: - **Justification for σ = 10:** Based on Hibbard et al. (2004) PMID 15333167, standard deviation of PAM-13 scores in similar populations ranges from 8 to 12; σ = 10 is the midpoint.
